1:
"And it came to pass in Iconium, that they went both together into the synagogue of the Jews, and so spake, that a great multitude both of the Jews and also of the Greeks believed." The witnesses note the success of the gospel at Iconium. The puritan Henry and the witnesses observe the apostles' regular method: entering the synagogue together, preaching, and winning a great multitude — both Jews and Greeks (the God-fearing Gentiles who attended the synagogue).

The grammatical voices note the effectiveness of the preaching: "so spake, that a great multitude believed." The manner of their speaking — bold, scriptural, Spirit-filled — was the means of the great success. The same message that won souls at Antioch of Pisidia (13:43) now wins both Jews and Greeks at Iconium.

The witnesses note the breadth of the conversion: Jews and Greeks alike. The gospel, preached first in the synagogue, drew not only Jews but the Greeks who gathered there — a foretaste of the wider Gentile harvest. The success, however, would provoke opposition (v.2), as it had at Antioch.

The thin comment matches the verse, which opens the Iconium narrative. The witnesses agree: at Iconium the apostles entered the synagogue together and so spake that a great multitude of both Jews and Greeks believed — following their regular method (Henry), the effective preaching winning a wide harvest. The breadth of the conversion (Jews and Greeks alike) displays the gospel's reach beyond Israel. The verse marks the success of the mission at Iconium: a great multitude won by the bold, scriptural preaching that had also prevailed at Antioch. The verse displays the power of the word effectively preached: the manner of the apostles' speaking, full of the Spirit, drew many to faith. Yet success and opposition go together in Acts: the great harvest at Iconium would at once provoke the unbelieving Jews to stir up the Gentiles (v.2). The verse sets the pattern of the mission — preaching in the synagogue, winning Jews and Greeks, and facing the consequent persecution. The gospel advances at Iconium, gathering a multitude of believers from both peoples, the firstfruits of the Gentile harvest that the journey would reap.
2:
"But the unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles, and made their minds evil affected against the brethren." The witnesses note the Jewish origin of the persecution. Ellicott observes the pattern: "It is the distinguishing feature of nearly all the persecutions in the Acts that they originated in the hostility of the Jews." The unbelieving Jews, unable to bear the gospel's success, incited the Gentiles against the disciples.

The grammatical voices note the embittering. Robertson: "to embitter, to exasperate." The Jews poisoned the minds of the Gentiles against Paul and Barnabas and the believers — turning those who might have heard the gospel into enemies of it.

The Reformed Calvin notes the Jews were like firebrands: "they were like firebrands to inflame the minds of the Gentiles." He notes the irony: the gospel, which ought to be the bond of unity, becomes (through men's perversity) the occasion of tumults. The "brethren" are all the godly, vexed and troubled for embracing the gospel.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the unbelieving Jews stirred up the Gentiles and embittered their minds against the brethren — the Jewish origin of nearly all the persecutions in Acts (Ellicott). The Jews, like firebrands (Calvin), poisoned the Gentiles against the disciples, turning potential hearers into enemies. The verse displays the opposition that the gospel's success provoked: the unbelieving Jews, galled by the conversion of Jews and Greeks (v.1), incited the Gentiles against the believers. The verse marks the rising persecution at Iconium, the counterpart to the success of v.1: where the gospel advances, opposition follows. The Jews who would not believe sought to prevent others from believing, embittering the Gentiles against the brethren. The verse displays the perversity that turns the gospel of peace into an occasion of strife — not by any fault of the gospel but by the malice of those who oppose it. The unbelieving Jews, unable to refute the truth, resort to inciting hatred — the pattern that would dog the mission and at last issue in the stoning of Paul (v.19). The verse displays the tragic root of the persecution: not honest objection to the gospel but the determination of the unbelieving to keep others from believing. Having lost the argument in the synagogue, they carry the fight outside it, poisoning the Gentiles who might otherwise have received the word. The gospel that gathered a multitude (v.1) now gathers, by the malice of its enemies, a hostile faction — yet the apostles, undaunted, abide and preach the longer (v.3).
3:
"Long time therefore abode they speaking boldly in the Lord, which gave testimony unto the word of his grace, and granted signs and wonders to be done by their hands." The witnesses note the bold perseverance of the apostles and the confirming miracles. On the long stay, Barnes notes they were not daunted by persecution: "Persecution and opposition may be attended often with signal success to the gospel." They remained, preaching boldly, because of the success God granted.

On the miracles, the witnesses note their purpose: to confirm the word. Ellicott: "here also, as so often elsewhere, the miracles that were wrought came as the confirmation of faith, not as its foundation." Robertson: "the Lord Jesus bore witness to the word of his grace, as he always does."

The Reformed Calvin draws out the true use of miracles — never separated from the word: "God doth never suffer them to be separated from his word... those miracles which came from God at any time did never tend to any other end but that the gospel might have his perfect and full authority." He uses this against the Papists, who lead men from the gospel by bare miracles.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles abode a long time, speaking boldly, undaunted by persecution because of God's success (Barnes), while the Lord confirmed the word of his grace with signs and wonders. The miracles came as the confirmation of faith, not its foundation (Ellicott); the Lord bore witness to his own word (Robertson). Calvin draws the true use of miracles: never separated from the word, but tending only to establish the gospel's authority — against the Papists who lead men astray by bare miracles. The verse displays the bold perseverance of the apostles and the divine confirmation of their message: God testifying to the word of his grace by miracles wrought through their hands. The verse marks the season of opportunity at Iconium: the apostles remaining, preaching boldly, confirmed by signs and wonders. The "word of his grace" — the gospel of God's mercy in Christ — is the message God attests; and the miracles serve only to commend it. The verse teaches that genuine miracles point to and confirm the word, and that any miracle (or claimed miracle) leading away from the gospel is to be rejected. God's signs and wonders established the bold preaching of his grace.
4:
"But the multitude of the city was divided: and part held with the Jews, and part with the apostles." The witnesses note the division of the city and the term "apostles." The grammatical voices note the schism. Robertson: "First aorist passive indicative of schizo... This division was within the Gentile populace." The Jewish leaders made some impression on the Gentiles, dividing the city.

On "the apostles," Robertson notes this is the first time Paul and Barnabas are so termed: "Certainly Luke does not here employ it in that technical sense" of the Twelve. The word means "one sent"; Paul claimed apostleship on a par with the Twelve (Galatians 1:1), but here it is used more broadly.

The Reformed Calvin reflects on the division as flowing from the gospel — yet not its fault: "so soon as any schism ariseth, before we condemn those who seem to be the authors, it behoveth us wisely to consider who ought to bear the blame." The Spirit pronounces the division to the praise, not the shame, of the apostles: better that a few depart from the world and come into favour with God than that, agreeing with the world, they have peace at the cost of disagreeing with God.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the city was divided — a schism within the Gentile populace (Robertson) — part holding with the Jews, part with the apostles. The term "apostles," first used here of Paul and Barnabas, is not in the technical sense of the Twelve but in the broader sense of those sent (Robertson). Calvin reflects on the division: it flowed from the gospel yet was not its fault, the blame lying with those who opposed the truth; and the Spirit pronounces it to the apostles' praise — better to divide from the world and come to God than to have peace with the world at the cost of God. The verse displays the inevitable division the gospel produces: where it is preached, men take sides, some for and some against. The verse marks the polarizing effect of the gospel at Iconium: the city split, the believers gathered to the apostles, the opposers to the Jews. The division, far from a reproach, is the necessary result of the gospel's challenge — for Christ came not to send peace but a sword (Matthew 10:34), dividing those who receive him from those who reject. The verse displays the cost and the clarity of the gospel: it divides, and the division reveals where each heart stands.
5:
"And when there was an assault made both of the Gentiles, and also of the Jews with their rulers, to use them despitefully, and to stone them," The witnesses note the planned violence against the apostles. The grammatical voices note the word "assault" (onset, impulse). Robertson: "It probably denotes not an actual attack so much as the open start, the co-operation of both Jews and Gentiles with their rulers." The "rulers" are those of the Jewish synagogue (Robertson).

On "despitefully" and "stone," Ellicott notes the connection to Paul's past: "St. Paul uses the noun derived from it to express the character of his own conduct as a persecutor (1 Timothy 1:13), and must have felt that he was receiving the just reward of his own deeds." The plan to stone them shows the Jews were in the lead, the intended punishment for supposed blasphemy.

The Reformed Calvin notes the apostles' courage and prudence: "They give not back when their enemies do only set themselves against them; but when the sedition waxeth hot, and they be in danger of stoning... they avoid the fury of the enemy. And though they fly, lest they throw themselves headlong into death, yet their constancy in preaching the gospel doth sufficiently declare that they feared not danger."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: an assault was planned — the co-operation of Gentiles and Jews with their (synagogue) rulers (Robertson) — to insult and stone the apostles. The intended stoning (the punishment for supposed blasphemy) shows the Jews in the lead; and Ellicott notes Paul, once a persecutor (1 Timothy 1:13), now received what he had dealt to others. Calvin notes the apostles' courage and prudence: they did not flee at mere opposition, but when in danger of stoning they avoided the fury — fleeing not from fear but in wisdom, their bold preaching elsewhere proving their courage. The verse displays the rising violence at Iconium: the opposition, no longer content with words, plans to stone the apostles. The verse marks the threat that drove them to Lystra (v.6) — the prudent flight that Christ had sanctioned (Matthew 10:23). The apostles, neither rashly courting death nor abandoning their duty, withdraw to preach elsewhere. The verse displays the balance of courage and prudence in the servants of Christ: to stand firm under opposition, yet to flee from needless death, that they may continue the work. The planned stoning at Iconium foreshadows the actual stoning at Lystra (v.19).
6:
"They were ware of it, and fled unto Lystra and Derbe, cities of Lycaonia, and unto the region that lieth round about:" The witnesses note the prudent flight to Lystra and Derbe. The grammatical voices note the obedience to Christ's command. Ellicott: "Here again, as in Acts 13:51, we can scarcely fail to trace a literal obedience to our Lord's commands (Matthew 10:23)." Robertson: "Paul and Barnabas had no idea of remaining to be stoned by this mob. It is a wise preacher who always knows when to stand his ground and when to leave."

On Lystra and Derbe, Ellicott describes the wild region of Lycaonia: "a dreary plain, bare of trees, destitute of fresh water... The very name Lycaonia, interpreted traditionally as Wolf-land... represented but too faithfully the character of the inhabitants." Here, for the first time, Paul had to begin his work by preaching to pure heathen, there being no synagogue.

The witnesses note the significance of the move into purely Gentile territory. Ellicott notes the converts of this region included Timothy of Lystra and Gaius of Derbe. The mission now entered a wilder, less civilized region, beyond the protection a Roman citizen might claim, and among genuine idolaters.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: aware of the plot, the apostles fled to Lystra and Derbe in Lycaonia — a literal obedience to Christ's command to flee persecution (Matthew 10:23, Ellicott), the wise preacher knowing when to leave (Robertson). The move took them into the wild region of Lycaonia ("Wolf-land," Ellicott), where, for the first time, Paul preached to pure heathen with no synagogue as a base. The converts here would include Timothy of Lystra and Gaius of Derbe (Ellicott). The verse displays the providence that turned persecution into the gospel's advance: driven from Iconium, the apostles carried the word into purely Gentile territory. The verse marks the extension of the mission into a wilder, less civilized region — the gospel now reaching genuine idolaters who had never had the Scriptures. The flight from Iconium became the means of bringing the gospel to Lystra, where the great healing and the refusal of worship (vv.8-18) would follow, and where Timothy would be won. Persecution scatters the seed; the gospel advances even where the apostles are driven.
7:
"And there they preached the gospel." The brief verse notes the continued preaching. The witnesses note the apostles' constancy: driven from Iconium, they did not rest or hide but preached the gospel at Lystra and Derbe. Gill: "they did not sit still, nor hide themselves... but, as in others, they preached the Gospel."

The grammatical voices note the extensive evangelistic work. Robertson: "We are to think of extensive evangelistic work perhaps with the assistance of disciples from Antioch and Iconium since Paul and Barnabas could not speak Lycaonian." The preaching at Lystra and Derbe was thorough, despite the language barrier (the locals spoke Lycaonian, v.11).

The continued preaching the witnesses read as the mark of the faithful evangelist. Persecution did not silence the apostles; it only moved them to a new field. The gospel that had been opposed at Iconium was now preached at Lystra and Derbe — the seed scattered by persecution taking root in new soil.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: at Lystra and Derbe the apostles preached the gospel — not resting or hiding but continuing their work (Gill), with extensive evangelism perhaps aided by disciples from the earlier cities (Robertson). The verse displays the constancy of the faithful evangelist: persecution at Iconium did not silence the apostles but moved them to a new field, where they preached the gospel afresh. The verse marks the gospel's advance into Lycaonia: the word preached at Lystra and Derbe, the seed scattered by persecution taking root in new soil. The verse, brief as it is, displays the unstoppable advance of the gospel: driven from one place, the apostles preach in another; opposed in one city, they carry the word to the next. The faithful servants of Christ do not abandon their work under persecution but continue it wherever they are driven. At Lystra the preaching would issue in the great healing (v.8) and the dramatic events that followed — the gospel bearing fruit even among the genuine heathen of Lycaonia. "And there they preached the gospel" is the unceasing refrain of the missionary apostles.
8:
"And there sat a certain man at Lystra, impotent in his feet, being a cripple from his mother's womb, who never had walked:" The witnesses note the introduction of the lame man at Lystra. The puritan Henry draws the parallel with Peter's miracle: "A miraculous cure wrought by Paul at Lystra upon a cripple that had been lame from his birth, such a one as was miraculously cured by Peter and John (Acts 3). That introduced the gospel among the Jews, this among the Gentiles."

The grammatical voices note the lifelong, hopeless nature of the affliction. The man was "a cripple from his mother's womb, who never had walked" — lame from birth, beyond all natural cure. As with the lame man at the Beautiful Gate, the chronic and incurable condition magnified the miracle to come.

The witnesses note the setting: the man "sat" at Lystra, perhaps to beg, where Paul was preaching. His lifelong lameness was well known, so that the cure could not be doubted or attributed to imposture. The verse introduces the great Lystra miracle that would lead the people to take the apostles for gods (v.11).

The thin comment matches the verse, which introduces the Lystra miracle. The witnesses agree: a man lame from birth, who had never walked, sat at Lystra where Paul preached — a lifelong, hopeless affliction beyond all natural cure. Henry draws the parallel: as Peter's healing of the lame man (Acts 3) introduced the gospel among the Jews, so Paul's healing here introduces it among the Gentiles. The man's well-known, incurable condition magnified the miracle to come, so that the cure could not be doubted. The verse displays the providential occasion for the manifestation of God's power at Lystra: a hopeless cripple, present where Paul preached, whose healing would astonish the heathen. The verse marks the parallel between Peter and Paul, the apostle to the circumcision and the apostle to the Gentiles, each healing a man lame from birth — the gospel attested by the same sign among Jews and Gentiles. The verse sets the stage for the great healing (vv.9-10) and the dramatic mistake that followed (vv.11-18): the cure of the lifelong cripple, displaying the power of God, would lead the Lystrans to take Paul and Barnabas for gods. The hopeless man awaits the word that will raise him.
9:
"The same heard Paul speak: who stedfastly beholding him, and perceiving that he had faith to be healed," The witnesses note the lame man's faith and Paul's perception of it. On the hearing, the witnesses note faith came by hearing. Clarke: "How did this faith come to this poor heathen? Why, by hearing the word of God preached." Ellicott: "he had for some days been among St. Paul's hearers, had heard the gospel of the death and resurrection of Jesus, and had found that such a Saviour met his every need."

On Paul's perceiving, the grammatical voices note the fixed gaze (atenizo, as in 13:9). Robertson: "Paul saw a new hope in the man's eyes and face." Ellicott: "All this the Apostle read, with that earnest gaze of his, in the man's upward look."

The Reformed Calvin notes this was a special gift-faith for the miracle: "this was a singular and extraordinary motion of the Spirit of God in the cripple... we must not make this a common rule, because the cripple believed that he should be healed, but it was a peculiar preparation to receive the gift of healing." The faith for the miracle was revealed to Paul by the Spirit.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the lame man heard Paul preach (faith coming by hearing, Clarke), having been among his hearers for some days (Ellicott), and Paul, fixing his gaze on him (as in 13:9, Robertson), perceived that he had faith to be healed. Calvin notes this was a special gift-faith for the miracle, not the ordinary saving faith — a peculiar preparation revealed to Paul by the Spirit. The verse displays the condition of the miracle (faith) and Paul's Spirit-given discernment of it: reading in the man's earnest upward look the faith that God had wrought. The verse marks the preparation for the healing: the man, having heard the gospel, believed he could be healed; and Paul, perceiving this by the Spirit, was about to speak the word of power. The verse displays both the role of faith in the miracle and the apostle's spiritual discernment: Paul saw, in the cripple's face, the hope that God had kindled. The healing would not be wrought on a doubter but on one prepared by the Spirit to receive it — faith the channel of the miraculous gift, discerned by the Spirit-filled apostle.
10:
"Said with a loud voice, Stand upright on thy feet. And he leaped and walked." The witnesses note the word of power and the immediate cure. On the loud voice, the witnesses note Paul spoke so all could hear. Calvin: "Paul exalted his voice, that the sudden change might the more move the multitude." The cure was public, that all might witness it.

On the textual addition, the witnesses note many MSS add "in the name of the Lord Jesus." Clarke: "it is not likely that St. Paul would not have used the sacred name on such an occasion." Calvin: "we see how careful the apostles were to magnify the name of Christ in all miracles." The cure was wrought in the name of Christ.

On the leaping and walking, the grammatical voices note the parallel with Acts 3:8. Robertson: "He leaped up with a single bound and began to walk." Ellicott notes the two tenses: "he leaped, as with a single bound, and then continued walking." The immediate, complete cure of one lame from birth displayed the power of God.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, with a loud voice (that all might hear and be moved, Calvin), commanded the cripple to stand, and he leaped up and walked — the immediate, complete cure of one lame from birth, parallel to Peter's miracle (Acts 3:8, Robertson). Many MSS add "in the name of the Lord Jesus" (Clarke, Calvin), the apostles careful to ascribe the cure to Christ. The verse displays the power of God in the apostolic miracle: the lifelong cripple, at the word of command, leaping up with a single bound and walking. The verse marks the great Lystra miracle: the hopeless man healed instantly, the power of Christ manifest among the heathen. The cure, wrought in the name of Jesus and witnessed by all, was unmistakably divine — and would lead the Lystrans, in their pagan understanding, to take Paul and Barnabas for gods (v.11). The verse displays both the reality of the miracle and its public character: a loud command, an instant cure, a man leaping and walking who had never walked. The power of God, displayed in the name of Christ, healed the cripple at Lystra — the sign that introduced the gospel among the Gentiles of Lycaonia.
11:
"And when the people saw what Paul had done, they lifted up their voices, saying in the speech of Lycaonia, The gods are come down to us in the likeness of men." The witnesses note the pagan response to the miracle. On "the speech of Lycaonia," Ellicott draws a key inference about the gift of tongues: "this serves almost as a crucial instance, showing that the gift of tongues, which St. Paul possessed so largely (1 Corinthians 14:18), did not consist in a supernatural knowledge of every provincial patois." The apostles did not understand the Lycaonian cry, hence did not at first grasp the people's intent.

On "the gods are come down," the witnesses note the pagan legend. Ellicott: "the form which the belief took was in accordance with the old legends of the district. There, according to the Myth which Ovid had recently revived, Zeus and Hermes (Jupiter and Mercury) had come in human guise." The Lystrans, genuine idolaters, supposed the gods had visited them again.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson on man's bent to superstition: "This history doth abundantly testify how ready and bent men are unto vanity... the people ascribe the praise of the miracle unto their idols, as if they had heard no word of Christ." He warns against catching at miracles while neglecting doctrine, the root of Popish superstition.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the people, seeing the miracle, cried out in Lycaonian that "the gods are come down in the likeness of men" — a pagan response shaped by the local legend of Zeus and Hermes visiting in human guise (Ellicott, from Ovid). Ellicott draws the crucial inference about the gift of tongues: the apostles did not understand the Lycaonian speech, proving the gift was not a supernatural knowledge of every dialect. Calvin draws the lesson on man's bent to superstition: the people ascribed the miracle to their idols, catching at the wonder while neglecting the doctrine — the root of all superstition. The verse displays the danger of miracles divorced from the word: the heathen, seeing the cure, fell at once into idolatry, taking the apostles for gods. The verse marks the dramatic misunderstanding at Lystra: the genuine idolaters, ignorant of the true God, interpreted the miracle through their own mythology. The verse also bears on the nature of the gift of tongues (it did not include every local patois) and on the perennial human tendency to corrupt the works of God into superstition. The miracle that should have led them to Christ led them, in their pagan darkness, to worship the messengers — a danger the apostles would at once confront (v.14).
12:
"And they called Barnabas, Jupiter; and Paul, Mercurius, because he was the chief speaker." The witnesses note the identification of the apostles with the pagan gods. On the assignment of names, the witnesses follow Chrysostom's conjecture about their appearance. Barnes: "Chrysostom's conjecture is, that Barnabas was a large, athletic man, and was hence taken for Jupiter; and Paul was small in his person, and was hence supposed to be Mercury." Ellicott notes Barnabas's "taller stature and more stately presence."

On "because he was the chief speaker," the grammatical voices note the role of Mercury (Hermes) as the messenger and god of eloquence. Robertson: "Mercury was the messenger of the gods, and the spokesman of Zeus... Because he was the chief speaker. Paul was clearly the leader of the talk." Since Paul did the speaking, he was taken for Mercury, the god of eloquence.

The witnesses note the pagan mythology behind the identification. Barnabas, the more imposing, was taken for Jupiter (Zeus), the father of gods; Paul, the eloquent speaker, for Mercury (Hermes), the divine messenger. The identification flowed from the local legend (v.11) and the apostles' respective appearance and role.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Lystrans called Barnabas Jupiter (Zeus) and Paul Mercury (Hermes) — Barnabas the more imposing taken for the father of gods (Chrysostom's conjecture, Barnes, Ellicott), and Paul, "the chief speaker," for the god of eloquence and the messenger of the gods (Robertson). The identification flowed from the local mythology (v.11) and the apostles' appearance and role. The verse displays the depth of the pagan misunderstanding: the heathen, interpreting the miracle through their own legends, assigned the apostles the names of their gods. The verse marks the dramatic irony at Lystra: the apostles who came to destroy idolatry are themselves taken for idols. The assignment of Mercury to Paul, "because he was the chief speaker," is a touching incidental detail — the people's own estimate of who led the mission. The verse displays both the genuine idolatry of the Lystrans and the danger the apostles faced: not persecution now, but the more insidious peril of being worshipped as gods. The misunderstanding, rooted in pagan darkness, would call forth the apostles' horrified refusal (v.14) and their sermon proclaiming the living God (vv.15-17).
13:
"Then the priest of Jupiter, which was before their city, brought oxen and garlands unto the gates, and would have done sacrifice with the people." The witnesses note the preparations for sacrifice and Luke's historical accuracy. On "Jupiter before the city," the witnesses note Luke's precision. Clarke: "Lystra, it appears, was under the guardianship of Jupiter Propulaius... which St. Luke translates, the Jupiter that was before the city, which is another term for Jupiter Custos, or Jupiter the guardian." Ellicott notes the identical phrase found in Greek inscriptions at Ephesus.

On the oxen and garlands, the witnesses note the sacrificial customs. Barnes: "The victims of sacrifice were usually decorated with ribands and chaplets of flowers." Clarke gives classical parallels (Ovid, Virgil). The oxen, garlanded for sacrifice, were brought to the gates.

The Reformed Calvin notes the priest's covetousness: "it is to be thought, that, forasmuch as there was great hope of most plentiful gain offered, he was moved with covetousness. For he had great hope of gain in time to come, if it should be noised abroad that Jupiter appeared there." The priest, scenting profit, led the people to sacrifice.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the priest of Jupiter (whose temple stood before the city, the guardian deity — Luke's precise phrase matching inscriptions, Clarke, Ellicott) brought garlanded oxen to sacrifice to the apostles. The sacrificial customs (the decorated victims) are noted from classical parallels (Barnes, Clarke). Calvin notes the priest's covetousness: scenting profit if it were noised that Jupiter had appeared, he led the people to sacrifice. The verse displays both Luke's historical accuracy (the guardian Jupiter before the city) and the depth of the idolatry: a priest preparing sacrifice to the apostles as gods. The verse marks the climax of the pagan misunderstanding: not merely calling the apostles gods (v.12) but preparing to offer sacrifice to them. The priest's role, perhaps driven by hope of gain (Calvin), shows how superstition is fed by self-interest. The verse displays the horror the apostles must confront: idolatrous worship offered to themselves, the very sin they came to abolish. The preparations for sacrifice — the oxen, the garlands, the gathered people — would call forth the apostles' violent protest (v.14) and their proclamation of the living God who alone is to be worshipped.
14:
"Which when the apostles, Barnabas and Paul, heard of, they rent their clothes, and ran in among the people, crying out," The witnesses note the apostles' horrified protest. Chrysostom marks the vehemence: "rent their clothes, ran in, cried out, all from strong affection of the soul, revolted by the things that were done. For it was a grief inconsolable, that they should needs be thought gods, and introduce idolatry, the very thing which they came to destroy!"

The grammatical voices note the renting of clothes — the Jewish expression of horror at blasphemy. The apostles, hearing of the intended sacrifice (which they had not at first understood, the cry being in Lycaonian, v.11), tore their garments and rushed in to stop the idolatry.

The witnesses note the contrast with later practice. The apostles' horror at being worshipped is a standing rebuke to all who receive such honour. They could not bear to be taken for gods, and rushed to prevent the sacrifice — the opposite of accepting divine honours.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles, hearing of the intended sacrifice (which they had not grasped at first, the cry being in Lycaonian), rent their clothes and rushed in among the people, crying out — the Jewish expression of horror at blasphemy (Chrysostom), revolted that they should be thought gods and made the occasion of idolatry, the very sin they came to destroy. The verse displays the apostles' vehement rejection of worship: not a calm demurral but a horrified protest, tearing their garments and rushing to stop the sacrifice. The verse marks the apostles' instant and total refusal of divine honours — a standing rebuke to all who would be worshipped or who accept the worship due to God alone. The contrast with Christ, who accepted worship (John 20:28), and with those who claim divine honours, is plain. The verse displays the holy horror of the true servants of God at idolatry, even when offered to themselves: they could not bear it, but rushed to prevent it, that God alone might be glorified. The apostles' renting of their clothes and crying out is the prelude to their proclamation of the living God (vv.15-17) — turning the people from worshipping the messengers to worshipping the Maker of heaven and earth.
15:
"And saying, Sirs, why do ye these things? We also are men of like passions with you, and preach unto you that ye should turn from these vanities unto the living God, which made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and all things that are therein:" The witnesses note the apostles' refusal and the beginning of their sermon to pagans. On "men of like passions," Barnes draws the great contrast with Christ: "When an offer was made to worship Paul and Barnabas, they shrank from it with strong expressions of indignation and abhorrence. Yet when similar worship was offered to the Lord Jesus... he commended the disciple (John 20:28)... How can this difference be accounted for, except on the supposition that the Lord Jesus was Divine?"

On "turn from these vanities," the witnesses note the term for idols. Ellicott: "the words vanity and vain were almost the invariable terms used by Jews to describe the emptiness and worthlessness of heathen worship." The apostles call the idols vanities and point to the living God.

The Reformed Calvin notes the method of preaching to pagans: beginning not with Christ directly but with the one Creator God, "which was not so far separate from common sense." He draws the general principle: "all religion is vain which departeth and degenerateth from the pure and simple Word of God."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles refused worship — "we are men of like passions with you" — and began their sermon to the pagans: turn from these vanities (idols) to the living God, Maker of all. Barnes draws the great contrast: the apostles abhorred the worship that Christ accepted (John 20:28), a proof of Christ's deity. The apostles call the idols "vanities" (the Jewish term for heathen worship, Ellicott) and proclaim the one Creator God. Calvin notes the method of preaching to pagans: beginning with the one Creator (near to common sense) before leading to Christ, and the principle that all religion departing from God's word is vain. The verse displays the apostles' refusal of worship and their proclamation of the living God: the messengers are men, the idols are vanities, and the true God is the Maker of heaven and earth. The verse marks the beginning of Paul's sermon to a purely pagan audience — distinct from his synagogue sermons (ch. 13), starting from creation and the one living God. The refusal of worship, grounded in the apostles' humanity and the deity of the God they preach, is the foundation of the gospel to the heathen: turn from dead idols to the living Creator. The contrast with Christ accepting worship silently attests his divinity.
16:
"Who in times past suffered all nations to walk in their own ways." The witnesses note Paul's philosophy of history regarding the heathen. The grammatical voices note "all nations" means all the Gentiles. Clarke: "all the Gentiles, merely to distinguish them from the Jewish people: who having a revelation, were not left to walk in their own ways." Ellicott: "We have here the first germ of what may be fairly described as St. Paul's philosophy of history. The times of ignorance had been permitted by God."

On God's "suffering" the nations, Barnes draws the purpose: the experiment of reason left to itself proved "the insufficiency of reason to guide men in the matters of religion... the need of revelation... the depravity of man." Robertson: "God's hand is on the history of all the nations, only with the Gentiles he withdrew the restraints of his grace in large measure."

The Reformed Calvin notes the apostles take away the cloak of long antiquity and universal consent: "let not the truth of God, when it appeareth, be less precious to you... seeing that it is eternal." The long blindness of the nations is no argument against the truth now revealed; rather, it shows the impotence of man's reason without God's word.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: God "in times past suffered all nations [the Gentiles] to walk in their own ways" — Paul's philosophy of history, the times of ignorance permitted by God (Ellicott, cf. Acts 17:30; Romans 1). God withdrew the restraints of his grace from the Gentiles (Robertson), the experiment of reason left to itself proving man's depravity and the need of revelation (Barnes). Calvin notes the apostles take away the cloak of long antiquity and universal consent: the long blindness of the nations is no argument against the now-revealed truth, but shows the impotence of man's reason without God's word. The verse displays the divine forbearance and its purpose: God permitted the nations to follow their own ways, leaving them to demonstrate the insufficiency of reason and the need of the gospel now preached. The verse marks Paul's address to the pagan past: the heathen had been left to walk in their own ways, yet not without witness (v.17). The "times of ignorance" were permitted by God, the nations following their own devices — until now, when the gospel calls them to turn to the living God. The verse displays God's patience and the failure of unaided reason: the long ages of pagan darkness, which the gospel now dispels, were the proof that men need revelation and grace to find the true God.
17:
"Nevertheless he left not himself without witness, in that he did good, and gave us rain from heaven, and fruitful seasons, filling our hearts with food and gladness." The witnesses note God's witness to the heathen in nature — natural revelation. On "left not himself without witness," the grammatical voices note this is the germ of Romans 1. Ellicott: "Here again we have the outline of what is afterwards expanded (Romans 1:19-20). In speaking to peasants like those at Lystra, St. Paul naturally dwells most on the witness given through the divine goodness as manifested in nature."

On the rhythmic close, Ellicott notes: "The latter words in the Greek are so distinctly rhythmical that they suggest the thought that St. Paul quotes from some hymn of praise which he had heard in a harvest or vintage festival." Robertson: "Paul does not talk about laws of nature as if they governed themselves, but he sees the living God behind the drama of the physical world."

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine of natural revelation and its limit: "this kind of testimony was such as that it made men without excuse, and yet was it not sufficient to salvation. For... faith is not conceived by the bare beholding of the heaven and earth, but by the hearing of the word." Nature's witness leaves men inexcusable but does not save.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God "left not himself without witness" — the witness of nature, his goodness in rain, fruitful seasons, food, and gladness (the germ of Romans 1, Ellicott). Paul, addressing peasants, dwells on God's goodness in nature; the rhythmic close suggests a quoted hymn of praise (Ellicott); and Paul sees the living God behind the physical world, not self-governing laws (Robertson). Calvin draws the doctrine and its limit: natural revelation leaves men without excuse, yet does not suffice for salvation, which comes by the word. The verse displays God's witness to the heathen: even in the times of ignorance (v.16), he testified to himself by his bounty — rain, harvest, food, and joy. The verse marks the heart of Paul's sermon to pagans: the one living God, Maker of all (v.15), who left himself not without witness in his daily goodness. The witness of creation and providence, sufficient to leave men inexcusable (Romans 1), points to the Creator whom the gospel now fully reveals. The verse displays God's universal goodness — the rain and fruitful seasons given to all — as the testimony that should have led the heathen to the true God, and now, with the gospel, calls them to turn to him. Natural revelation witnesses; the gospel saves.
18:
"And with these sayings scarce restrained they the people, that they had not done sacrifice unto them." The witnesses note the difficulty of stopping the idolatrous worship. The grammatical voices note "scarce restrained." Robertson: "It had been a harrowing and well-nigh a horrible ordeal, but finally Paul had won." Barnes: "They were so fully satisfied that the gods had appeared, and were so full of zeal to do them honour."

On the fruit of the sermon, Ellicott notes some may have been converted: "On some of those who were thus restrained the effect may well have been that they were roused to a higher life and did turn from vanities to the living God." Among the converts at Lystra were Timothy and his mother Eunice and grandmother Lois (2 Timothy 1:5).

The Reformed Calvin notes the madness of idolatry: "how mad and untamed the heat of the world is toward idolatry. For if they believe the gods, why do they not believe their word, whereby they put from them false honor?" The people's zeal for sacrifice, scarcely restrained even by the apostles' words, shows the obstinacy of superstition.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: with their sermon (vv.15-17), the apostles scarce restrained the people from sacrificing to them — a harrowing ordeal, the people so convinced the gods had appeared and so zealous to honour them (Barnes, Robertson). Calvin notes the madness of idolatry: the people would not believe the apostles' word though they believed them gods, the obstinacy of superstition. Yet Ellicott notes some may have been truly converted, including Timothy and his family (2 Timothy 1:5). The verse displays both the difficulty of overcoming idolatry and the partial success of the gospel: the apostles barely stopped the sacrifice, yet some hearts were turned. The verse marks the close of the Lystra worship scene: the apostles, by their vehement refusal and their proclamation of the living God, just managed to prevent the idolatrous sacrifice. The difficulty shows the deep hold of superstition; the partial success shows the power of the gospel even among genuine heathen. The verse displays the apostles' faithful struggle against idolatry, even when offered to themselves, and the beginning of a church at Lystra — for from this work would come Timothy, Paul's beloved son in the faith. The gospel, scarcely restraining the idolaters, nonetheless turned some from vanities to the living God.
19:
"And there came thither certain Jews from Antioch and Iconium, who persuaded the people, and, having stoned Paul, drew him out of the city, supposing he had been dead." The witnesses note the sudden reversal — from worship to stoning. The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the fickleness of the crowd. Chrysostom: "the Gentiles regarded them as gods, but these dragged him out of the city, supposing he had been dead... In the very city in which they received this reverence, in the same were they thus terribly ill treated."

On the Jewish pursuit, the witnesses note the malice. Chrysostom: "children of the devil, that not in their own cities only, but also beyond them, they did these things." The Jews from Antioch and Iconium followed the apostles to Lystra to oppose them, persuading the fickle crowd to stone Paul.

On the stoning, the witnesses note its severity. Wesley describes the Jewish manner of stoning. The crowd that had nearly worshipped Paul now stoned him and dragged him out for dead — a striking instance of the fickleness of popular favour and the persistence of Jewish malice.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Jews from Antioch and Iconium came and persuaded the fickle crowd, who stoned Paul and dragged him out for dead — a sudden reversal from worship to stoning (Chrysostom), in the very city where he had nearly been worshipped. The Jewish malice pursued the apostles even beyond their own cities (Chrysostom), and the crowd's fickleness turned adoration into violence. The verse displays the persistence of opposition and the instability of popular favour: the people who would have sacrificed to Paul now stoned him. The verse marks the climax of the persecution on the first journey: Paul stoned at Lystra, the fulfillment of the suffering foretold at his call (9:16) and the very fate he had dealt to Stephen (7:58). The verse displays both the malice of the unbelieving Jews, who followed the apostles to oppose them, and the fickleness of the crowd, swayed from worship to murder. Yet the stoning, intended to end Paul's work, would not: he rose up and continued (v.20). The verse marks the cost of the gospel — Paul left for dead — and the providence that preserved him. The apostle who had stoned Stephen is himself stoned, and survives to preach again.
20:
"Howbeit, as the disciples stood round about him, he rose up, and came into the city: and the next day he departed with Barnabas to Derbe." The witnesses note Paul's recovery — and divide on whether it was miraculous. The witnesses divide. Many hold it a miraculous recovery, near a resurrection. Clarke: "Miraculously restored, not only to life, but to perfect soundness." Wesley: "a miracle little less than a resurrection from the dead." Calvin: "It appeareth plainly that Paul was miraculously saved, seeing that, on the morrow, after he was cast out for dead, he taketh his journey, being fresh and sound."

Barnes, however, holds it a natural recovery: "The probability is, that he was stunned by a blow... and after a short time recovered from it. Nothing is more common than thus by a violent blow on the head to be rendered apparently lifeless, the effect of which soon is over." He objects that the text does not assert a miracle.

On the disciples and Paul's courage, the witnesses note the touching scene. Robertson: "Timothy may very well have been along... Timothy, a lad of about fifteen, would not soon forget that solemn scene (2 Timothy 3:11)." Calvin marks Paul's invincible courage: he returns to the very city, then journeys on to Derbe.

The witnesses agree: as the disciples stood round (perhaps to bury him, or hoping for revival), Paul rose up, entered the city, and the next day departed to Derbe. They divide on the recovery: many hold it miraculous, near a resurrection (Clarke, Wesley, Calvin — that he journeyed fresh and sound the next day proving the miracle); Barnes holds it a natural recovery from a stunning blow, the text not asserting a miracle. Either way, the witnesses marvel at Paul's courage in entering the very city where he was stoned and journeying on. Timothy, perhaps present, would never forget the scene (Robertson, 2 Timothy 3:11). The verse displays the preservation and the courage of the apostle: stoned and left for dead, he rose and continued the work. The verse marks the survival of Paul and the indomitable spirit of the gospel: persecution could not silence him. Whether by miracle or by God's providence in a natural recovery, Paul was preserved for further labour — entering the city again, then pressing on to Derbe. The verse displays the cost and the triumph of the mission: the apostle, stoned for the gospel, rises to preach again, his courage undimmed. The God who let Paul be stoned preserved his life, that the work might continue and the door of faith be opened to the Gentiles.
21:
"And when they had preached the gospel to that city, and had taught many, they returned again to Lystra, and to Iconium, and Antioch," The witnesses note the work at Derbe and the courageous return. On Derbe, the grammatical voices note it was the frontier city, where the apostles made many disciples. Robertson: "Paul and Barnabas were literally here obeying the command of Jesus in discipling people in this heathen city."

On the return, the witnesses marvel at the courage. Barnes: "We have here a remarkable instance of the courage of the apostles. In these very places they had been persecuted and stoned, and yet in the face of danger they ventured to return... The welfare of the infant churches they deemed of more consequence than their own safety." Clarke: "Behold the courage of these Christian men!"

The witnesses note the purpose of the return: to confirm the disciples (v.22). The apostles, having reaped a harvest at Derbe, retraced their steps through the cities of persecution to strengthen the new converts — placing the welfare of the churches above their own safety.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles preached at Derbe and made many disciples (obeying Christ's command to disciple the nations, Robertson), then returned through Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch — the very cities of persecution. The witnesses marvel at their courage: they ventured back into danger, deeming the welfare of the infant churches above their own safety (Barnes, Clarke). The verse displays both the fruitful work at Derbe and the courageous, pastoral return: the apostles retracing their steps to confirm the new converts. The verse marks the turning-point of the journey: having reached Derbe, the apostles begin the return, revisiting the churches to strengthen them. The courage to return to the places of persecution displays the apostles' devotion: they cared more for the disciples than for their own safety. The verse displays the pastoral heart of the mission: not content with founding churches, the apostles return to establish them, even at the risk of their lives. The return through the cities of persecution, to confirm the disciples (v.22), shows that the work of the gospel is not only to win converts but to build them up. The apostles, having reaped, return to nurture the harvest.
22:
"Confirming the souls of the disciples, and exhorting them to continue in the faith, and that we must through much tribulation enter into the kingdom of God." The witnesses note the strengthening of the disciples and the necessity of affliction. On "confirming," the witnesses guard against the ecclesiastical rite. Barnes: "It has no reference to any imposition of hands... It means simply that they established, strengthened, made firm, or encouraged by the presentation of truth." Ellicott prefers "strengthening" to avoid the later associations.

On "much tribulation," the witnesses note the necessity of affliction for Christians. Calvin: "we must go on warfare in this world, that we may live well and godly... to the end we may persist even unto the end, we must be prepared for war." He guards against the Papist reading of tribulation as meritorious: "their babbling is frivolous who gather hereby that patience is a work which deserveth eternal salvation, seeing that the cause of salvation is not in this place handled, but after what sort God useth to handle his."

On the "we," Ellicott and Robertson note it may reflect Luke's own hearing or a general truth. Robertson: "It does not follow from this use of 'we' that Luke was present, since it is a general proposition applying to all Christians (2 Timothy 3:12)."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles strengthened the disciples and exhorted them to continue in the faith, teaching that "we must through much tribulation enter the kingdom of God." "Confirming" is not the ecclesiastical rite but simply strengthening by the truth (Barnes, Ellicott). The necessity of affliction — that the Christian life is warfare, requiring preparation for tribulation (Calvin) — is the sober counsel given the new converts; yet tribulation is not meritorious but the way God handles his own (Calvin, against the Papist reading). The "we" may reflect Luke's hearing or a general truth (Ellicott, Robertson, 2 Timothy 3:12). The verse displays the apostles' pastoral care: strengthening the disciples and preparing them for the suffering that attends the gospel. The verse marks the realistic counsel of the mission: the new converts, having seen Paul stoned, are taught that tribulation is the appointed path to the kingdom. The verse displays both the encouragement and the warning of the gospel: the way to glory lies through affliction. Paul, who had just suffered stoning, teaches the disciples to expect the same, that they may not be discouraged but may persevere. The necessity of tribulation, far from a deterrent, is the appointed road to the kingdom — and the apostles, by their own example, confirm it.
23:
"And when they had ordained them elders in every church, and had prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord, on whom they believed." The witnesses note the appointment of elders and debate the word "ordained." On "ordained" (cheirotoneo), the witnesses note its meaning of election by show of hands. Barnes: "The word properly denotes to stretch out the hand; and as it was customary to elect to office, or to vote, by stretching out the hand, so the word simply means to elect, appoint, or designate." Clarke distinguishes cheirotonia (election by show of hands) from cheirothesia (ordination by imposition of hands), holding the people elected and the rulers appointed.

The witnesses divide on whether the people elected. Ellicott: "Used, as it is here, of the act of the two Apostles, not of the Church, the latter meaning [appointment] seems most in harmony with the context." Calvin and Robertson hold the people elected (by suffrage) and the apostles presided: Calvin: "in ordaining pastors the people had their free election, but lest there should any tumult arise, Paul and Barnabas sit as chief moderators."

On the office and the prayer, the witnesses note the elders (= bishops) on the synagogue model (Robertson), and the solemnity of prayer and fasting. The apostles, about to leave, committed the churches to elders and commended all to the Lord.

The witnesses agree: the apostles appointed elders in every church, with prayer and fasting, and commended them to the Lord. On "ordained" (cheirotoneo), the witnesses debate: the word properly means election by show of hands (Barnes, Clarke distinguishing it from ordination by imposition of hands); some hold the apostles simply appointed (Ellicott), others that the people elected by suffrage while the apostles presided (Calvin, Robertson). The elders (= bishops) were on the synagogue model (Robertson), and the appointment was solemnized with prayer and fasting. The verse displays the apostles' care for the permanence of the churches: appointing elders to oversee them, and commending all to the Lord on whom they believed. The verse marks the organization of the Gentile churches: the apostles, about to depart, provide for the ongoing ministry by appointing elders, with prayer and fasting. The debate over "ordained" touches the question of church polity — whether the people elected or the apostles appointed — but the witnesses agree on the substance: spiritual oversight provided for each church, the work solemnized by prayer, and the churches committed to the Lord's keeping. The verse displays the wisdom of the apostles in establishing, not merely founding, the churches — providing elders, seeking God's blessing, and entrusting the new believers to Christ.
24:
"And after they had passed throughout Pisidia, they came to Pamphylia." The brief verse notes the continuation of the return journey. The grammatical voices note the route: through Pisidia (where Antioch was) to Pamphylia (toward the coast). Robertson: "After leaving Antioch they passed on through Pisidia and into Pamphylia."

The witnesses note the apostles' continued evangelism. Calvin: "we may thereby guess that they had not opportunity offered them everywhere to teach, which they were wont to neglect or let pass nowhere." Where they could, they preached; where they could not, they passed on.

The verse marks a stage in the homeward journey: the apostles, having confirmed the churches (vv.22-23), now travel toward the coast and home. The return retraces the outward route, completing the circuit of the first missionary journey.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles passed through Pisidia to Pamphylia (Robertson), continuing the homeward journey and preaching where opportunity offered (Calvin). The verse marks a stage in the return: having confirmed the churches, the apostles travel toward the coast, retracing the outward route. The verse, brief as it is, displays the steady progress of the homeward journey: the apostles moving through the regions they had evangelized, toward the port and home. The verse continues the record of the first missionary journey's completion: the circuit nearly closed, the apostles returning to report. The verse displays the diligence of the apostles even on the return: passing through the regions, preaching where they could (Calvin), not neglecting any opportunity. The journey that began at Antioch (13:1) now nears its end, the apostles having planted and confirmed churches across Cyprus and Asia Minor. The verse marks the penultimate stage: through Pisidia and Pamphylia toward Perga and Attalia (v.25), and thence home to Antioch (v.26). The first great missionary journey, with all its triumphs and trials, draws to its close. The verse displays the unhurried faithfulness of the apostles even on the homeward leg: they do not rush to safety but move through the regions they had evangelized, ready to preach wherever a hearing is given. The God who sent them (13:4) now brings them safely back, the work fulfilled and the door of faith opened to the Gentiles. The brief notice of the route is itself a quiet testimony to the completeness of the journey and the providence that guarded it from Cyprus to the highlands of Asia Minor and back again.
25:
"And when they had preached the word in Perga, they went down into Attalia:" The witnesses note the preaching at Perga and the journey to the port. Ellicott notes a coincidence: on the outward journey (13:13) there was no mention of preaching at Perga (where John Mark left them); now, on the return, they did what they had left undone. "On their return, accordingly, they did what they had then left undone. They went down into Attalia."

The grammatical voices note Attalia as the port of Pamphylia, from which they would sail to Antioch. The apostles, having preached at Perga, went down to the coast to find a ship home.

The witnesses note the apostles' diligence in preaching even at the journey's end. Where on the outward journey Perga had been passed by, on the return it received the word. The apostles neglected no opportunity to preach the gospel.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles preached the word at Perga (doing on the return what they had left undone on the outward journey, where John Mark had departed, Ellicott) and went down to Attalia, the port of Pamphylia, to sail home. The verse displays the apostles' diligence in preaching even at the journey's end: Perga, passed by before, now received the word. The verse marks the final stage of the journey before the voyage home: the apostles preaching at Perga, then descending to the port. The verse displays the completeness of the apostles' work: not neglecting Perga on the return, but preaching there before embarking. The first missionary journey nears its close: the gospel preached across the regions, the churches confirmed, and the apostles now heading for the coast and home. The verse, brief as it is, shows the faithful diligence of the apostles — preaching wherever they went, even on the homeward leg, leaving no opportunity unused. From Attalia they would sail to Antioch (v.26), completing the circuit and bringing the report of the door of faith opened to the Gentiles (v.27). The journey ends as it advanced — with the preaching of the word.
26:
"And thence sailed to Antioch, from whence they had been recommended to the grace of God for the work which they fulfilled." The witnesses note the return to Antioch and the completion of the work. On "recommended to the grace of God," the witnesses note this refers back to the sending (13:1-3). Barnes: "Where they had been appointed to this missionary tour by the church." The apostles had been commended to God's grace for the work, and now returned having fulfilled it.

On "the work which they fulfilled," Barnes draws conclusions: it was a missionary tour (now accomplished), not an ordination to the ministry or apostleship (Paul was an apostle before, Barnabas never in the technical sense): "It was a designation to a temporary work, which was now fulfilled." Wesley: "This shows the nature and design of that laying on of hands (13:3)."

The Reformed Calvin notes the ascription to God's grace: "their whole journey, together with the success, was committed to God, the author thereof. Therefore, their preaching was no man's work, but a work of the grace of God." The success of the mission is ascribed wholly to God's grace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles sailed to Antioch, whence they had been commended to the grace of God for the work now fulfilled (referring back to the sending, 13:1-3, Barnes). The "work fulfilled" was the missionary tour, now accomplished — not an ordination to the ministry or apostleship but a temporary commission completed (Barnes, Wesley). Calvin ascribes the whole journey and its success to God's grace: their preaching was no man's work but God's. The verse displays the completion of the first missionary journey: the apostles returning to their sending church, the work fulfilled by the grace of God. The verse marks the close of the journey: the apostles home at Antioch, the mission accomplished, the success ascribed to God. The verse displays the faithfulness of the apostles (they fulfilled the work) and the grace of God (who enabled and prospered it). The journey that began with the church's commendation to grace (13:3) ends with the work fulfilled — the door of faith opened to the Gentiles (v.27). The verse displays both the completeness of the apostles' obedience and the sovereignty of God's grace in the mission: they fulfilled the work, but it was God's work, accomplished by his grace through their faithful labour. The first missionary journey closes with the apostles home and the gospel advanced.
27:
"And when they were come, and had gathered the church together, they rehearsed all that God had done with them, and how he had opened the door of faith unto the Gentiles." The witnesses note the first missionary report and the great phrase "the door of faith." On the gathering and report, Robertson: "It was the first missionary meeting in history... It was a long story for they had many things to tell of God's dealings with them." The apostles, as returning ambassadors, reported to the sending church.

On "all that God had done with them," the witnesses note the apostles ascribed all to God, not themselves. Calvin: "Not that they did extol the things which they themselves had done, but whatsoever things the Lord had done by them." The report magnified God's grace.

On "the door of faith," the witnesses note Paul's characteristic metaphor. Ellicott: "It would seem to have been a favourite metaphor of St. Paul's (1 Corinthians 16:9; 2 Corinthians 2:12; Colossians 4:3)." Calvin notes the door was opened not only by the external preaching but by the effectual inward call: "no man entereth in save he to whom God reacheth out his hand; no man draweth near unless he be drawn inwardly by the Spirit."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles gathered the church and reported "all that God had done with them" — the first missionary meeting in history (Robertson) — ascribing all to God, not themselves (Calvin), and how God had "opened the door of faith unto the Gentiles." The "door of faith" is Paul's characteristic metaphor (Ellicott, 1 Corinthians 16:9); and the door was opened not only by external preaching but by the effectual inward call of the Spirit (Calvin). The verse displays the apostles' faithful report and the great achievement of the journey: the door of faith opened to the Gentiles, the gospel now manifestly for all nations. The verse marks the climax of the first missionary journey: the apostles' report magnifying God's grace, and the recognition that God had opened the door of faith to the Gentiles. The phrase, Paul's own, expresses the epochal significance: the Gentiles now enter the kingdom through the door of faith, not by law or circumcision. The verse displays both the humility of the apostles (ascribing all to God) and the magnitude of the work (the Gentile door opened). The first missionary journey ends with the church rejoicing in what God had done — the gospel's advance to the nations, the door of faith flung open to all who believe.
28:
"And there they abode long time with the disciples." The brief verse closes the chapter with the apostles' rest at Antioch. The grammatical voices note the long stay. Chrysostom (under v.14): "For the city being great had need of teachers." The apostles remained at Antioch, their home base, ministering to the church.

The witnesses note the rest after the labours and dangers of the journey. After the trials of the first missionary journey — the persecutions, the stoning, the perils — the apostles abode a long time with the disciples at Antioch, a season of fellowship and ministry before the next stage (the Jerusalem council, ch. 15).

The long stay the witnesses read as a time of consolidation. The apostles, having reported the opening of the door of faith (v.27), remained to teach and strengthen the church at Antioch, the great Gentile church that was the base of the mission. The verse closes the account of the first missionary journey.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: the apostles abode a long time with the disciples at Antioch — a season of rest, fellowship, and ministry after the labours and dangers of the journey (Chrysostom: the great city needed teachers). The verse displays the apostles' continued ministry at their home base: after the trials of the mission, they remained to teach and strengthen the church. The verse marks the close of the first missionary journey: the apostles home at Antioch, abiding with the disciples, the great Gentile church consolidated. The verse, brief as it is, completes the record of the journey that opened the door of faith to the Gentiles. The long stay at Antioch was a time of consolidation before the next great event — the Jerusalem council (ch. 15), where the question of the Gentiles' admission without circumcision would be settled. The verse displays the rhythm of the apostolic mission: labour and rest, journey and abiding, the apostles ministering to the church between the journeys. The first missionary journey ends with the apostles at home, the church strengthened, and the gospel advanced to the nations — the door of faith opened, and a long season of ministry at Antioch before the work would resume.
